coredata.dc:title: Quantification of alkaline phosphatase in cell lysates by a simple fluorescence method in combination with a modified standard addition calibration strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Min-Xi
Chen, Yao
Chen, Zeng-Ping
Yu, Ru-Qin
coredata.dc:description: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an important diagnostic indicator of many human diseases. Its detection is of great importance in fields such as clinical diagnosis and biomedical research. In this contribution, a simple label-free fluorescence sensing method for ALP detection was designed by integrating ALP triggered hydrolysis, terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase assisted DNA polymerization and complex formation between G-quadruplex and thioflavin T together. Furthermore, in order to mitigate the influence of matrix effects and background interference effects on the quantitative results of the proposed fluorescence sensing method encountered in the analysis of real-world complex biological samples, the probe technique-based generalized multivariate standard addition strategy (GMSAprobe) was specifically modified, and combined with the proposed fluorescence sensing method to achieve satisfactory quantitative results for ALP in cell lysate samples with accuracy comparable to that of a commercial ALP assay kit. Its limit of detection and limit of quantification values were about 0.02 and 0.07 U/L, respectively, more than 20 times lower than those of the commercial ALP assay kit. Due to its merits of simplicity, sensitivity, specificity, as well as robustness to matrix effects and background interference effects, the combination of the proposed fluorescence sensing method and the modified GMSAprobe has a great potential for ALP assay in biochemical research and clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkaline phosphatase
Cell lysates
Fluorescence
Probe technique-based generalized multivariate standard addition strategy
Matrix effects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Working mechanism of the fluorescence sensing method based on terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase and thioflavin T for ALP detection
Feasibility study of the propose method
Quantification of ALP in cell lysates
Evaluation of the efficacy of ALP inhibitors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Working mechanism of the fluorescence sensing method based on terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase and thioflavin T for ALP detection.

Fluorescence emission spectra of different mixtures (a: DNA primer and ThT; b: DNA primer, ALP and ThT; c: DNA primer, TdT and ThT; d: DNA primer, TdT, inactivated ALP and ThT; e: DNA primer, TdT, ALP and ThT. The concentrations of DNA primer, TdT, ALP and ThT were 1 ​μM, 0.3 U/μL, 0.5 U/L, and 50 ​μM, respectively).

Results of gel electrophoresis (Lane 1: solution of DNA primer; Lane 2: mixture of DNA primer and ALP; Lane 3: mixture of DNA primer and TdT; Lane 4: mixture of DNA primer, TdT and ALP. The concentrations of DNA primer, ALP and TdT were 2.5 ​μM, 5 U/L and 0.2 U/μL, respectively).
The influence of dephosphorylation reaction time on the fluorescence intensity at 495 ​nm (Error bars showed the standard deviation of three experiments).
The influence of the amount of TdT on the fluorescence intensity at 495 ​nm (Error bars showed the standard deviation of three experiments).
The influence of polymerization reaction time on the fluorescence intensity at 495 ​nm (Error bars showed the standard deviation of three experiments).
The fluorescence responses of samples with different ALP concentrations measured at 495 ​nm.
The fluorescence responses at 495 ​nm obtained by the proposed method for samples of different proteins (BSA, HSA, human IgG, lysozyme and trypsin: 0.5 ​μM; ALP: 0.01 ​μM).
The fluorescence spectra of a standard sample of ALP (solid line) and three cell lysate samples (97H: long dash line, U251: dotted line, HepG2: dash-dot-dot line) without the addition of DNA primer.

a) Fluorescence spectra of samples containing different amounts of Na3VO4; b) The relationship between the inhibition rate of Na3VO4 and its concentration (the ALP concentration used in the inhibition experiment was 0.5 U/L).
The concentrations of ALP in cell lysate samples of 97H, U251 and HepG2 determined by different methods, respectively.

head.ce:title: Quantification of alkaline phosphatase in cell lysates by a simple fluorescence method in combination with a modified standard addition calibration strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, Hunan, 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, Hunan, 410082, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Lab of Biomedical Materials and Devices, College of Life Sciences and Chemistry, Hunan University of Technology, Zhuzhou, 412008, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Lab of Biomedical Materials and Devices
College of Life Sciences and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Key Lab of Biomedical Materials and Devices, College of Life Sciences and Chemistry, Hunan University of Technology, Zhuzhou, 412008, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an important diagnostic indicator of many human diseases. Its detection is of great importance in fields such as clinical diagnosis and biomedical research. In this contribution, a simple label-free fluorescence sensing method for ALP detection was designed by integrating ALP triggered hydrolysis, terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase assisted DNA polymerization and complex formation between G-quadruplex and thioflavin T together. Furthermore, in order to mitigate the influence of matrix effects and background interference effects on the quantitative results of the proposed fluorescence sensing method encountered in the analysis of real-world complex biological samples, the probe technique-based generalized multivariate standard addition strategy (GMSAprobe) was specifically modified, and combined with the proposed fluorescence sensing method to achieve satisfactory quantitative results for ALP in cell lysate samples with accuracy comparable to that of a commercial ALP assay kit. Its limit of detection and limit of quantification values were about 0.02 and 0.07 U/L, respectively, more than 20 times lower than those of the commercial ALP assay kit. Due to its merits of simplicity, sensitivity, specificity, as well as robustness to matrix effects and background interference effects, the combination of the proposed fluorescence sensing method and the modified GMSAprobe has a great potential for ALP assay in biochemical research and clinical diagnosis.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Probe technique-based generalized multivariate standard addition strategy

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an enzyme widely distributed in liver, bones, intestines, kidneys, and placenta [1]. It can catalyze the removal of an phosphate group from the 3′ terminus of a nucleic acid molecule, thereby converting the 3′-PO4 terminus of a DNA or RNA fragment to a 3′-OH terminus [2]. Aberrant expression of ALP is related to many diseases, such as bone disease, liver dysfunction, diabetes, breast and prostatic cancer [3–5]. Accurate detection of ALP is essential for clinical diagnosis. Moreover, the content of ALP is also widely used as an index for monitoring eutrophication of sediments and water in field of environmental biology [6–8]. Therefore, simple, convenient, highly specific and sensitive methods for ALP detection are highly desired.

Recent years, various methods [9–12] for ALP assay have been developed. Among them, fluorescence sensing methods [13–16] with the advantages of rapidity, high sensitivity and good selectivity have gained more and more attention. All kinds of novel fluorescence probes such as quantum dots [17,18], noble metal nanoclusters [19–21], and near-infrared fluorescence probe [22] have been reported for the detection of ALP. For instance, Qu et al. proposed the combination of carbon dots (CDs) and MnO2 nanosheets for ALP detection [17]. The fluorescence of CDs could be effectively quenched by MnO2 nanosheets through fluorescence resonance energy transfer. ALP can catalyze the hydrolysis of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) to ascorbic acid (AA) which can reduce MnO2 to Mn2+ resulting in ALP concentration related fluorescence recovery of CDs. Wang et al. reported a similar method for ALP detection based on the flower-like cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) and copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) aggregates [19]. AA produced by the ALP-triggered hydrolysis of AAP can reduce CoOOH sheets to Co(II) ions, and hence the fluorescence of CuNCs quenched by CoOOH sheets is recovered. The content of ALP can be detected indirectly by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs. A hemicyanine-based near-infrared fluorescent probe named CyP was first synthesized and used to detect ALP activity by Li et al. [22] Though these fluorescence sensing methods boast of high sensitivity and selectivity, they are generally liable to the influence of matrix effects from non-fluorescent compounds (which may enhance or suppress fluorescent intensities of samples) and background interference effects from fluorescent compounds other than the analytes of interest or fluorescence probes added in real-world complex samples. Hence, there is a need to improve the quality of quantitative results of fluorescence sensing methods for ALP in complex samples with both matrix effects and background interference effects.

Recently, some of the present authors proposed a probe technique-based generalized multivariate standard addition strategy (GMSAprobe) for the analysis of fluorescence signals with matrix effects [23]. GMSAprobe has a great potential to improve the accuracy and precision of fluorescence sensing methods for quantitative analysis of complex samples. In this contribution, a simple but effective fluorescence sensing method based on terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT) and thioflavin T (ThT) for ALP detection was proposed. And the GMSAprobe was specifically modified to address the problem of matrix effects encountered in the quantification of ALP in cell lysates using the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working mechanism of the fluorescence sensing method based on terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase and thioflavin T for ALP detection
ce:section.ce:para: TdT is a template-free polymerase that can catalyze the random addition of deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates (dNTP) to the 3′-OH terminus of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) or 3′-protruding double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) fragments [24]. It has been widely used as a versatile tool for the detection of DNA [25], RNA [26], small molecular [27], metal ions [28], and functional enzymes [29,30], etc. Here, a simple but effective label free fluorescence sensing method for ALP detection was designed by taking advantage of TdT-assisted DNA polymerization as shown in Fig. 1 . First of all, a DNA primer with phosphoryl group at its 3′ terminal is designed as the substrate of ALP-catalyzed hydrolysis. In the presence of ALP, the 3′-phosphoryl group of the DNA primer can be hydrolyzed to a hydroxyl group, which will initiate the elongation reaction of the DNA primer with the addition of TdT and dGTP [31,32]. The resultant elongated DNA can readily fold into G-quadruplexes under the inducement of K+. ThT can specifically bind with G-quadruplexes to form G-quadruplex-ThT complexes [33,34], resulting in a strong increase in fluorescence emission centered at 495 ​nm. While in the absence of ALP, the DNA primer with its 3′ terminal passivated by a phosphoryl group can’t trigger the TdT-assisted DNA polymerization process and the subsequent formation of G-quadruplex-ThT complexes. No obvious increase in fluorescence emission will be observed. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of G-quadruplex-ThT complexes reflects the concentration of ALP in a sample.

To address the problem of matrix effects encountered in the quantification of ALP in complex biological samples using the proposed fluorescence sensing method, GMSAprobe was specifically modified as follows. Suppose row vector
x i
(i ​= ​1, 2, …, N) is the fluorescence spectrum measured for the i-th standard sample, it can be expressed as the linear combination of the contributions of the G-quadruplex DNA-ThT complex and possible background interference(s).
(1)
x i
=
c
D N A − T h T , i
·
r
D N A − T h T
+
b
s t a n d
Where
c
D N A − T h T , i
is the concentration of the complex formed between G-quadruplex DNA and ThT in the reaction mixture of the i-th sample.
r
D N A − T h T
represents the fluorescence response of the DNA-ThT complex of unit concentration. b
stand denotes the background interference(s) in the standard samples, which is constant for all standard samples. Since the higher the concentration of ALP in the i-th sample, the more the elongated DNA will be produced, and the higher the concentration of DNA-ThT complex. The concentration of DNA-ThT complex (
c
D N A − T h T , i
) in the reaction mixture of the i-th sample is therefore linearly related to ALP concentration (
c
A L P , i
) in the i-th sample.
c
D N A − T h T , i
=
k 1

A L P , i

A multivariate linear calibration model can be built between

· β , i ​= ​1, 2, …, N) by multivariate regression methods such as partial least squares (PLS) [35].

Similarly, the fluorescence spectra of a real test sample and its standard addition sample can be expressed as follows:

Where, x

add are the fluorescence spectra of the real test sample and its standard addition sample, respectively.
c
A L P , t e s t
is the concentration of ALP in the real test sample. Δ c is the concentration of ALP spiked into the standard addition sample. b
test denotes the fluorescence contribution of possible background interference(s) in the test sample, which might be different from b
stand due to the difference in the background interferences between the real test sample and the standard samples.
g
is a positive parameter accounting for matrix effects on the spectral signals caused by the differences in matrices between the real test sample and the standard samples.

Since the fluorescence signal related to the concentration of ALP in both the standard samples and the real test sample is generated from the addition of all the indispensible ingredients of the proposed method (e.g., DNA primer, TdT, ALP and ThT), the background spectra (i.e., b
stand and b
test ) of the standard samples and the real test sample can be measured before the addition of any one of the non-fluorescence indispensible ingredients. Suppose the predictions of the PLS model from (b

.

The DNA primer (5′-AACTATGCAACCTACTACCTCT-phosphate-3′) used in this work was commercially synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), 10 ​× ​AP Buffer (1 ​M Tris-HCl, 100 ​mM MgCl2, 100 ​mM KCl, 0.1 ​mM ZnCl2, pH 8.0), 100 ​mM dGTP, lysozyme, trypsin, and thioflavin T (ThT) were all obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd.. Terminal transferase (TdT), 10 ​× ​TdT buﬀer (500 ​mM KAc, 200 ​mM Tris-Ac, 100 ​mM MgAc2, pH 7.9), and 2.5 ​mM CoCl2 solution were purchased from New England Biolabs (Beijing, China). Human IgG, Na3VO4, Stains-All (~95%), TEMED (BioReagent, suitable for electrophoresis, ~99%), and 30% acrylamide/bis-acrylamide solution (BioReagent, suitable for electrophoresis) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human serum albumin (HSA) was provided by Yuan Ye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris, 99.9%), ammonium persulfate (APS, 99.9%), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid calcium disodium salt hydrate (BioReagent, suitable for electrophoresis, 99%), and potassium chloride were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Boric acid was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. ALP assay kit was supplied by Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise stated, all chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Watson’s distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

For each of the three types of cells (i.e., 97H, U251 and HepG2), 5 ​× ​106 ​cells were collected into a tube by trypsinization, and mixed with 3 ​mL of sterile physiological saline solution. The resulting mixture was subsequently centrifuged at 1000 ​rpm for 5 ​min. The supernatant was discarded. The cells in the tube were washed once with 3 ​mL of physiological saline solution, dispersed in 1 ​mL of deionized water, and stored in a refrigerator at −80 ​°C. After thawing, the cells were completely lysed for 5 ​min followed by centrifugation at 8000 ​rpm for 10 ​min. The resultant supernatant was decanted to a new tube.

Standard samples with eleven different concentration levels of ALP (i.e., 0, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40, 0.50, 0.60, 0.70, 0.80, 0.90, and 1.00 U/L) were prepared on ice by mixing 5 ​μL solution of DNA primer (10 ​μM), 5 ​μL 10 ​× ​AP Buffer and appropriate volume of ALP working solution (2.00 U/L), and diluting to a final volume of 50 ​μL with sterilized water.

The lysate solutions of 97H and U251 were diluted 10 times with sterilized water, while the lysate solution of HepG2 cells was diluted 50 times. After dilution, for each of the three types of cells, one real test sample and one standard addition sample were prepared. The real sample was prepared by mixing 5 ​μL solution of DNA primer (10 ​μM), 5 ​μL 10 ​× ​AP Buffer, 5 ​μL of the corresponding diluted cell lysate solution and diluting to a final volume of 50 ​μL with sterilized water. The standard addition sample was prepared by mixing 5 ​μL solution of DNA primer (10 ​μM), 5 ​μL 10 ​× ​AP Buffer, 5 ​μL of the corresponding diluted cell lysate solution, an appropriate volume of ALP working solution (2.00 U/L) and diluting to a final volume of 50 ​μL with sterilized water. The volumes of ALP working solution added into the standard addition samples of 97H, U251 and HepG2 were 7.5 ​μL, 7.5 ​μL and 5 ​μL, respectively. Three blank solutions of 50 ​μL for the three types of cell lysates samples were prepared by mixing 5 ​μL 10 ​× ​AP Buffer, 5 ​μL corresponding diluted cell lysate solution and appropriate amounts of sterilized water, respectively. Each sample had three replicates.

All samples were incubated at 37 ​°C for 90 ​min followed by heat inactivation at 75 ​°C for 15 ​min. After being cooled to room temperature, each sample was mixed with 1.5 ​μL TdT (20 U/μL), 10 ​μL 10 ​× ​TdT reaction buffer, 10 ​μL CoCl2 (2.5 ​mM), and 3.5 ​μL dGTP (100 ​mM), and diluted to a final volume of 100 ​μL with sterilized water. The mixture was incubated at 37 ​°C for 2 ​h and then inactivated at 75 ​°C for 20 ​min. Subsequently, a solution of 50 ​μM ThT prepared in 1 ​× ​ThT reaction buffer (10 ​mM Tris-HCl with 50 ​mM KCl, pH 7.2) was added to yield a final volume of 200 ​μL. After incubation at room temperature for 30 ​min, the resulting reaction mixture was subjected to fluorescence measurement and gel electrophoresis.

Fluorescence emission spectrum of the reaction mixture of each sample was recorded at a resolution of 1 ​nm over the range of 460–650 ​nm by an FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edingbergh Instruments Ltd., UK) equipped with a continuous xenon arc lamp. The excitation wavelength was set at 435 ​nm. A scanning speed of 240 ​nm/min was employed. The slit widths of excitation and emission were fixed at 2.0 and 4.0 ​nm, respectively. Each sample was analyzed three times.

A 15% polyacrylamide gel was prepared with 3 ​mL distilled water, 4 ​mL 30% acrylamide/bis-acrylamide solution, 0.8 ​mL 10 ​× ​TBE buffer (0.89 ​M Tris-boric acid, 20 ​mM EDTA), 20 ​μL APS and 15 ​μL TEMED. 10 ​μL of the reaction mixture of each sample along with 2.5 ​μL glycerin was loaded onto the polyacrylamide gel and then run in 1 ​× ​TBE buffer at 110 ​V for 1 ​h. The gel was stained with Stains-All for 10 ​min and imaged with a mobile phone camera.

The standard samples with ALP contents of 0, 0.20, 0.40, 0.60, 0.80 and 1.00 U/L were designated as the calibration set. The rest standard samples made up the test set. For each of the three types of cells, a calibration model was built on the whole fluorescence spectra of the calibration set by the modified GMSAprobe. The model parameters of the GMSAprobe models (i.e., the optimal number of underlying components in the calibration model and the value of the parameter representing the matrix effects in the real test samples) were optimized and determined according to the criterion of minimum mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of the corresponding standard addition sample and the test set. For comparison purpose, the commonly used univariate calibration model was established between the concentrations of ALP in the standard samples and their corresponding fluorescence intensities at 495 ​nm. A partial least squares regression model was also built on the whole fluorescence spectra of the calibration set. The optimal model parameter of the PLS calibration model was optimized in terms of the smallest root-mean-square error (RMSE) for the test set. In order to validate the accuracy of quantitative results of the method developed in this contribution, the concentrations of ALP in the undiluted cell lysate samples were also determined by a commercial ALP assay kit (Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility study of the propose method
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, a set of experiments were carried out to verify the feasibility of the proposed fluorescence sensing method for ALP detection. As shown in Fig. 2 , the addition of either ALP or TdT into the mixture of the DNA primer and ThT caused no obvious changes in its fluorescence emission spectrum. Only when all the four main indispensable ingredients (i.e, DNA primer, TdT, ALP and ThT) were present in the reaction mixture, did a significant increase in fluorescence emission occur. In order to further confirm that the increase in fluorescence emission was directly related to the hydrolysis activity of ALP, ALP was heated at 95 ​°C for 15 ​min before incubation with DNA primer, TdT and ThT. Clearly, no obvious increase in fluorescence emission was observed when active ALP was replaced by heat-inactivated ALP, because heat-inactivated ALP could not trigger the dephosphorylation of DNA primer and the subsequent polymerization reaction. Gel electrophoresis was also performed to verify polymerization reaction initiated by ALP. As shown in Fig. 3 , only the band of DNA primer was observed for solution of DNA primer (Lane 1), mixture of DNA primer and ALP (Lane 2), and mixture of DNA primer and TdT (Lane 3), indicating that neither ALP nor TdT alone can trigger the polymerization reaction of DNA primer. When both ALP and TdT were present (Lane 4), the color of the DNA primer band became lighter, and overlapping bands of DNA sequences longer than DNA primer appeared. All these experimental results confirmed the feasibility of the proposed fluorescence sensing method for ALP detection.

Fig. 7 displayed the fluorescence responses of samples with different ALP concentrations measured at 495 ​nm. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity at 495 ​nm increased monotonically with the increase of ALP concentration in the range from 0.005 to 2.00 U/L. Further increase of ALP concentration above 2.00 U/L saw no obvious increase in fluorescence intensity. Within the concentration range of 0.01–1.00 U/L, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity at 495 ​nm and the ALP concentration approximately followed a linear model. As a result, a dynamic linear range in the region of 0.10–1.00 U/L was obtained.

BSA, HSA, IgG, lysozyme and trypsin were used as potential interferences to test the selectivity of the proposed method for ALP. The concentration of each of the potential interferences was kept at 0.5 ​μM. And their fluorescence responses were compared with that of 0.5 U/L ALP (equivalent to 0.01 ​μM). As shown in Fig. 8 , only ALP could induce a significant increase in the fluorescence intensity at 495 ​nm. The addition of all the other proteins considered in this work resulted in no obvious increase in the fluorescence intensity, indicating the high selectivity of the proposed method for ALP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of ALP in cell lysates
ce:section.ce:para: Since it is difficult, if not impossible, to prepare standard samples with exactly the same media as that of cell lysate samples, possible background effects and matrix effects might significantly affect quantitative results for ALP. Fig. 9 showed the fluorescence spectra of a standard sample of ALP and three cell lysate samples without the addition of DNA primer (which is an indispensible ingredient of the proposed method). It was clear that significant differences existed among the background spectra of the standard sample and cell lysate samples. This was the exact reason that the proposed method was combined with the probe strategy based standard addition strategy to quantitative analyze ALP in cell lysate samples.

Table 1 listed the concentrations of ALP in the undiluted cell lysate samples determined by the proposed method in combination with the univariate model, PLS model and modified GMSAprobe model, respectively. Please note that the concentrations of ALP in the undiluted cell lysate samples were calculated by multiplying the predictions of these calibration models for the corresponding real test samples by the corresponding dilution factors. For the purpose of verification and comparison, the concentrations of ALP determined by the commercial ALP assay kit were also provided in the table. As expected, the univariate calibration model commonly used in the field of biosensors could not provide satisfactory predictions for ALP in real-world cell lysate samples. For the sample with relatively lower ALP concentration, its prediction value was negative, and therefore was marked as “not detected”. Due to the multivariate nature of the PLS calibration model, its performance was generally better than that of the univariate calibration model. At least, its prediction values were all positive, though some of them were significantly different from the corresponding expected values. Clearly, because of the presence of background effects and matrix effects demonstrated in Fig. 9, neither univariate calibration model nor the PLS model could provide satisfactory quantitative results for ALP in all the three cell lystae samples. In comparison, the quantification results of the proposed method in combination with the modified GMSAprobe model were in good consistent with the corresponding values determined by the commercial ALP assay kit. The relative differences between the results of these two methods varied from −14.8% to 18.3%, which were deemed acceptable considering the complexity of the real-world cell lne samples. All these results signified that the proposed method integrated with the modified GMSAprobe model could achieve the same level of accuracy as the commercial ALP assay kit during the determination of ALP in complex cell lysate samples. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of the proposed method for ALP were estimated to be 0.02 and 0.07 U/L, respectively, more than 20 times lower than those of the commercial ALP assay kit. So a much smaller volume of sample is needed for ALP detection using the proposed method, which poses a significant advantage for applying the proposed method to fields such as clinical diagnostics, biomedical research, etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the efficacy of ALP inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: As a common ALP inhibitor, Na3VO4 was used to test the performance of the proposed method for evaluating of the efficacy of ALP inhibitors. Na3VO4 can inhibit ALP activity, suppress the dephosphorylation reaction of the DNA substrate, and hence prevent the subsequent polymerization reaction, resulting in the decrease of fluorescence intensity at 495 ​nm. As can be seen in Fig. 10 , the fluorescence intensity at 495 ​nm decreased proportionally to the increase of Na3VO4 concentration. When the concentration of Na3VO4 increased up to 50 ​μM, its inhibition efficiency reached saturation. These results suggest that the proposed method is capable of evaluating the efficiency of ALP inhibitors, and is applicable to the screening of ALP inhibitors in drug discovery.

In this study, a simple but effective fluorescence sensing method was proposed for ALP detection based on terminal transferase and thioflavin T. The proposed method has a high sensitivity and selectivity for ALP. However, when applied to the quantification of ALP in complex biological samples such as cell lysates, its quantitative results are liable to the influence of matrix effects and background interference effects. With a view to improve the accuracy and precision of its quantitative results, the probe technique-based generalized multivariate standard addition strategy (GMSAprobe) was specifically modified for analyzing the fluorescence spectral data obtained by the proposed fluorescence sensing method. The integration of the proposed fluorescence sensing method with the modified GMSAprobe achieved satisfactory results for ALP in three types of cell lysate samples. The relative differences between the results of the proposed method and the corresponding valued determined by a commercial ALP assay kit were within the range from −14.8% to 18.3%. The proposed method has great potential in the quantitative assay of ALP in clinical diagnostics as well as the screening of ALP inhibitors in drug discovery.

Min-Xi Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yao Chen: Investigation, Validation, Visualization. Zeng-Ping Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ru-Qin Yu: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21775038, no. 21705044), and the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (no. 2018JJ3114).
